steps.text: Submit a copy, either electronic or paper, of any test report from application of this OTM to EPA’s Measurement Technology Group.
Submit electronic copies via email with the subject line “OT

Configure the VFC Canister Sampling System with Water/Acid Gas Management by connecting a heated transfer probe, a heated filter, an impinger conditioning system, an unheated transfer line, and the unheated critical orifice and connecting fittings to a bypass pump.
Extract and deliver a representative gaseous sample from the source to the critical orifice using the bypass pump.
Direct a portion of the sample gas to a passivated canister through the outlet of the critical orifice.
Vent excess source gas from the bypass pump back to the stack or vent, or treat it to remove VOC and vent to the atmosphere.
Optionally add the Multiple Canister configuration to the train equipment configurations to collect paired samples or varied sample volumes.
Open the canister valve, record the start time/start timer, and collect the sample for approximately 60 minutes.
Collect the sample gas at a minimum of at least three discreet duct or stack traverse points, sampling for an equal amount of time at each traverse point.
Close the canister valve, record the stop time, perform a post-test leak check, and disconnect the canister from the train.
Confirm the final canister vacuum.
Attach the vacuum gauge to the canister and open the canister valve.
Record the final pressure.
Compare the final pressure and associated sample volume (Eq. 19) to the theoretical volume sampled from the sample time and orifice flow rate (Eq. 20).
Close the valve, remove the gauge, and replace the canister inlet cap.
Setup, heat, and leak check the sampling system as described in Sections 8.5 and 8.6.
Connect and deliver a flow of UHP nitrogen or zero air to the inlet of the sample probe at a flow rate slightly exceeding that of the sampling system bypass flow rate.
Purge the system for at least two minutes.
Collect the sample as described in Section 8.6.
Collect a 4 to 5 L sample in a 6 L canister.
Submit the canister
Collect the sample as described in Section 8.6. Collect a 4 to 5 L sample in a 6 L canister.
Submit the canister for analysis as a sample.
Check the field integrity of collected samples.
Perform the comparison of sample volumes required in Section 8.6.2.8.
Flag data that differ between the two approaches by ≥20% RPD and indicate if leakage is suspected and invalidate the sample with a similar explanation.
Check the laboratory integrity of collected field samples.
Upon receipt in the laboratory perform the comparison of sample volumes required in Section 11.1.2.
Flag data that differs between the two approaches by more than 5% RPD and indicate leakage is suspected or invalidate the data with a similar explanation.
Provide COC information.
Perform gas dilution system evaluations by generating and analyzing blank samples using humidified zero air.
Perform mass flow or pressure gauge audits on a yearly basis.
Assess the results from the lowest calibration concentration canister sample and determine if it meets accuracy criteria of ±30% of each target compound spike concentration.
Perform the procedures outlined in the IDC (Section. 9.2.3) and verify that all QC acceptance criteria are met.
Record and archive results in tables with associated QC narratives demonstrating acceptable performance criteria.
Perform LB analysis.
Prepare and analyze an LB at the beginning of every sample sequence.
Verify LB/artifact levels meet the requirements in Table OTM-50-4.
If the LB does not meet requirements, perform corrective actions, and re-analyze an LB to ensure QC requirements are met.
Analyze the LB using the same concentrator instrument settings and GC/MS operating conditions as used for the ICAL and field samples.
Use standard gaseous mixtures with certified concentrations of IS compounds in ultrapure nitrogen.
Evaluate the IS upon receipt for the presence of contaminants that may interfere with the quantitation of target compounds.
Prepare a humidified CCV standard in a canister at a concentration in the lower third of the calibration curve.
Prepare the CCV standard independently from a secondary certified calibration standard (if available), or from pure/neat compounds if unavailable.
Analyze a CCV standard to verify the ICAL for each target compound.
Analyze a daily CCV.
Prepare the VFC calibration standards using procedures in Sections 10.1-10.3.
Prepare analytical sequence for instrument calibration.
Run one LB prior to the calibration standards.
Establish the ICAL using individual standards in separate canisters at each level of the calibration curve and injecting the same volume from each canister.
Run calibration standards from the lowest to highest concentration.
Analyze a LB after the highest calibration standard.
Run a CCV after the LB.
Review all retention times, chromatographic peak shapes, and integrations for the calibration standard compounds to ensure proper quantification of VFCs.
Adjust analytical method parameters to remedy any observed issues and rerun and/or reprocess data from 10.7.3 as necessary.
Generate a calibration curve for each VFC target compound.
Calculate the Static Dilution (Cf) using Equation 8.
Calculate the Average Relative Response for a Target Compound (RRS) using Equation 10.
Calculate the Dilution factor for sample pressurization (DFC) using Equation 11.
Calculate the Relative Response Factor (RRF) using Equation 12.
Calculate the Instrument-Detected Analyte Concentration (CD) using Equation 13.
Calculate the Concentration, in ppb, of the Target Compound in canister as received Stack Gas (Car) using Equation 15.

preconditions.text: Emissions stream stack moisture is >3% v/v or acid gas levels are a concern.
For anticipated high VFC concentrations that require dilution for analysis
At least one sampling system background determination must be performed for each test campaign.
The sampling system background sample must be collected in the field prior to the collection of actual samples.
At the beginning of each analytical sequence
If high concentration standards or samples have been run, instrument blanks may be run before the LB to flush the system.
Once the calibration standards are analyzed

constraints.text: Gas samples from stationary combustion sources contain significant quantities of CO2 and condensed water or water vapor which interfere with VFC compounds in this method.

Water in the sample gas was found to coelute with analytes on certain chromatography columns used to evaluate this method and affected the response of those analytes. Poor or inconsistent water management during preconcentration can cause peak broadening and retention time shifts.
Acid gases and water condensed in canisters can result in corrosion of the interior surface of the canisters, resulting in reactive surfaces that compromise target compounds.
CO2 in the collected sample may coelute and/or interfere with the quantitation of target compounds in this method.
If coeluting compounds have common ions, the common ions should not be used as the target’s quantifying or qualifying ions.
Contamination in the sampling system and canisters may interfere if sampling equipment is not pr
Connection tubing for the static dilution system must be constructed of chromatographic-grade stainless-steel (minimum type 316).
Carrier gas lines supplying the GC must be constructed from clean stainless-steel or copper tubing with non-polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) thread sealants.
Flow controllers, cylinder regulators, or other pneumatic components fitted with rubber components are not suitable.
A minimum of three discreet duct or stack traverse points are required to collect a representative sample.
A RPD > 20% indicates a potential leak or a clogged orifice and requires resampling.
The pressure should be approximately 17-34 kPa (5-10 in. Hg) vacuum for a 4 to 5 L gas sample collected in the 6 L canister.
For high concentration small volume canister samples, volume differences >20% RPD may be acceptable due to differences in pressure measurement equipment.
Gas dilution systems used to generate calibration gases shall be capable of producing standards whose measured values are within ±3 percent of predicted values.
The blank volume used for analysis must match the nominal volume of sample to be analyzed.
IDC results must be made available upon request.
Do not use IS gas standards that fail the LB acceptance criteria.
Each IS compound in each sample injection must be within ±5 seconds of the mean RT for each IS compound in the most recent calibration.

The area response for each IS compound in each injection must be within ±40% of the mean area response of the IS compound determined from the ICAL determined using Eq. 10 in Section 12 or the most recent calibration check, whichever is most appropriate.
The CCV standard must contain all compounds in the calibration mixture.
Recovery of each target compound in the CCV standard must be within ±20% of the theoretical concentration.
The difference between the quantitated concentrations of the target compounds (%Dccv) and the theoretical concentrations should be within ±20% using Eq. 3 in section 12.
LB concentrations are acceptable if they are <3x MDL or ≤ 50 % of the project required reporting limit for each target compound, whichever is higher.

Due to the similar boiling points of many of the compounds in the target list, it may not be possible to chromatographically resolve all of them. Unique quantifying and qualifying ions should be selected, if possible.
Manual integration may be used to correct for improper integrations performed by the software but must be flagged for data points in the calibration curve.
Average Retention Time (RT) is not used in the current method.
Instrument Dilution Correction Factor (DFI) is not used in this method.
